eli3: Dependency parsing analyzes the grammatical structure of a sentence by identifying binary head-dependent relations between words. Each word (except root) has exactly one head — the word it depends on — and a labeled dependency relation (subject, object, modifier, etc.). 'The dog chased the cat' → chased ← dog (nsubj), chased → cat (obj), dog ← The (det), cat ← the (det).

The dependency tree is a rooted directed tree covering all tokens. Universal Dependencies (UD) defines a cross-lingual tagset of ~40 relation types (nsubj, obj, iobj, nmod, amod, det, aux, cc, etc.) used across 100+ languages and 200+ treebanks. The UD representation focuses on content words as heads (unlike constituency-based phrase structure).

Modern dependency parsers use neural transition-based (spaCy, arc-eager/arc-standard automata) or graph-based (biaffine parser, Dozat & Manning 2017) approaches. Graph-based parsers score all possible arcs independently and find the maximum spanning tree (MST) — state-of-the-art. UAS (unlabeled attachment score) and LAS (labeled attachment score) are the standard metrics.

whenWhy.title: Extracting semantic relationships for IE
whenWhy.body: Dependency parses make syntactic relationships explicit: 'The CEO of Apple, Tim Cook, announced…' → parsing reveals Tim Cook as nsubj of 'announced' and appositive of CEO. Open IE systems (Stanford OpenIE, AllenNLP) use dependency paths to extract (subject, relation, object) triples without predefined schemas. Biomedical relation extraction uses dependency paths between entity mentions.

whenWhy.title: Semantic role labeling prerequisite
whenWhy.body: SRL (who did what to whom, when, where) uses dependency structure to identify argument spans. Dependency paths between a predicate and its arguments serve as features in SRL models. Syntactic structure constrains which noun phrases can be subjects or objects of a given verb — helping resolve argument ambiguity.

whenWhy.title: Cross-lingual NLP via Universal Dependencies
whenWhy.body: UD's language-universal representation enables cross-lingual transfer: a parser trained on English UD can transfer to a new language using multilingual BERT features, even without target-language treebanks. UD also enables typological studies — comparing head-directionality (SVO vs SOV languages), relative clause placement, and adposition placement across language families.

deepDiveIntro: A dependency parse is a directed tree where each word points to its head — the word it grammatically depends on. An arc is projective if it does not cross any other arc when drawn above the sentence, which is the case for most English sentences. Languages with flexible word order (German, Czech) have more non-projective arcs, requiring parsers that can handle crossing dependencies.

Transition-based parsers work like a stack machine: a classifier decides at each step whether to push the next word onto a stack, draw an arc between the top two items, or pop the stack. The arc-eager system by Nivre (2003) processes a sentence of n words in exactly n transitions, making it very fast for real-time use.

deepDive.heading: Biaffine Graph-Based Parser (Dozat & Manning 2017)
deepDive.body: The biaffine parser scores every possible arc between every pair of words independently using a learned scoring function that combines the head word's representation and the dependent word's representation. After scoring all arcs, it finds the highest-scoring valid tree using the Chu-Liu/Edmonds maximum spanning tree algorithm, then labels each arc in the tree. This globally-optimal decoding approach achieves a labeled attachment score of about 95% on English. [1]

deepDive.heading: Evaluation: UAS and LAS
deepDive.body: Unlabeled attachment score (UAS) measures what fraction of tokens have the correct head word. Labeled attachment score (LAS) additionally requires the dependency label to be correct. Both exclude punctuation tokens by convention. State-of-the-art parsers on Universal Dependencies English reach about 95% UAS and 93% LAS. The remaining errors cluster around classic syntactic ambiguities — prepositional phrase attachment, coordination scope, and relative clause placement — where even human annotators sometimes disagree. [1]

deepDive.heading: Transition-Based vs Graph-Based Parsers
deepDive.body: Transition-based parsers make greedy left-to-right decisions and process a sentence in linear time — a small early mistake can propagate to later decisions, though beam search reduces this. Graph-based parsers score all possible arcs independently and then find the globally best tree, which is more accurate but requires quadratic time in sentence length. In practice, transition-based parsers are preferred for real-time applications where speed matters, and graph-based for offline tasks where accuracy is paramount. [1]

deepDive.heading: Dependency vs Constituency Parsing
deepDive.body: Constituency parsing groups words into nested phrases with explicit phrase nodes (NP, VP). Dependency parsing captures direct head-to-word relationships without phrase nodes — it tells you who depends on whom without constructing the full phrase hierarchy. Dependency representations are easier to interpret for semantic tasks like information extraction, more consistent across languages, and more natural for word-level neural models. Constituency trees are more useful when explicit phrase boundaries matter, such as span-based NER or syntactic reordering in machine translation. [1]

takeaways: Dependency tree: each word has one head; labeled arc captures grammatical relation (nsubj, obj, amod…). [1]
Biaffine parser: scores all arcs with biaffine attention, finds MST — state-of-the-art (LAS ~93% English UD). [1]
Evaluation: UAS (head correct), LAS (head + label correct), excluding punctuation. [1]
Transition-based O(n): fast, greedy, error propagation; graph-based O(n²): globally optimal, slower. [1]
Universal Dependencies (UD): ~40 cross-lingual relation types, 200+ treebanks — enables cross-lingual transfer. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does dependency parsing handle non-projective arcs?
edgeCases.a: Non-projective arcs cross other arcs when drawn above the sentence — common in free word order languages (Czech, German) and in coordination. Transition-based arc-eager cannot produce non-projective trees; requires the SWAP transition (Nivre 2009) or Attardi's non-projective transition system. Graph-based MST parsers naturally handle non-projectivity since they score all arcs independently. For English (mostly projective), the projectivity assumption costs <1% LAS; for Czech, ~10% of arcs are non-projective. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between dependency parsing and SRL?
edgeCases.a: Dependency parsing is syntactic: it captures grammatical relations (nsubj = grammatical subject, not necessarily the agent). SRL is semantic: it identifies thematic roles (Agent, Patient, Instrument) relative to a predicate, following PropBank or FrameNet schemas. A word can be the nsubj of a passive verb but have the Patient role in SRL ('The cat was chased by the dog' → cat is nsubj syntactically but Patient semantically). Modern SRL models use dependency structure as features but ultimately make semantic predictions. [1]

sources.label: Jurafsky & Martin — Speech and Language Processing (3rd ed.), Ch. 18
Dozat & Manning (2017) — Deep Biaffine Attention for Neural Dependency Parsing, ICLR 2017
